utterances: She says it's brought a feeling of great relief.
This was her first time.
It won't be her last.
(Speaking Portuguese).
I'll be back,"" she says.
Philip Reeves, NPR News, Sao Paolo.
You're listening to Talk of The Nation, Science Friday.
I'm Ira Flatow and for the rest of the hour we're going to be talking about the night sky, and I'm really jealous of our Science Friday crew.
Last night they got out to see the night sky and the beautiful stars and the nebulas, and planets that they could see.

And one of the - well, my most favorite things and you won't find a better view of the night sky's from here in Arizona because you have the clear weather, it's dry, the moisture is gone, you have the dark skies and this desert south, east, and along with some high mountain peaks.
It's a ideal occasion for looking out at the stars, but forget about binoculars and the sky chart because we're not talking about taking out those binoculars, you don't really need to do that here.

Let's all look at the constellations, because you can actually go out and look at some of the world's best telescopes, look through the telescope yourself and you can see the globular clusters, the white dwarfs and the far away galaxies and all it takes is one visit to Arizona's World Class Observatories where they have these public viewing programs.
I'll give you a chance to spend an evening, you can even overnight there sometimes with a professional astronomer, who's going to guide you through the milky way and beyond.

And I'm talking with me - joining me now to talk more about this are my guests Adam Block, is the public observing programs coordinator for the University of Arizona's, Mount Lemmon SkyCenter, he's also an astrophotographer.
Thank you for being with us today.
Hi, Ira.
Douglas Isbell, is the associate director of Public Affairs for the Kitt Peak National Observatory, also co-chair of the U.S.
Program Committee, an International year of astronomy 2009.
Thank you for being with us here today.
Let's talk, Doug, why is there so much astronomy going on here in Arizona?
Is it basically the weather so, so great?
Yes, it's basically the climate.

The clear dry skies during the day and night and of course they also wanted to be close to an air port, close to a major university those are some considerations, so it was actually 50 years ago this year that Kitt Peak was elected to be the side of the National Observatory on the land of (unintelligible).
And so, we have sort of a neat mission.
We're the open astronomy center for any astronomer in the world ready to apply and come observe and do their science.
And how can we amateurs get involved?
We have a very popular evening program.
It's a four hour program where you really gets take your time and look at the night's sky.
And Adam worked in that program and help develop it and I was taking it too - a new location and there was some new twists.
We have an overnight program for two people to enjoy the rest of the night all by themselves with the guide.
Those are two good ways and then we're doing all kinds of things next year to help bring new people into astronomy.
Mm hmm.
Adam, tell us about the Mount Lemmon SkyCenter?
The Mount Lemmon SkyCenter is really a premium place to take advantage of the skies and the conditions that you were talking about.
It is effectively a way to access the universe in Tucson's backyard.
It's the tall mountain that rises 9,000 feet right behind Tucson.
1-800-989-8255 here in the audience step up to the mike if you'd like to ask about observing the skies, it may be your only shot at talking to the astronomers before we get there.
And Mount Lemmon man was actually a military operation first, was it not?

That's right, there are still the old cold war relics that still stand there and remind us of what times used to be, there's a very tall radar facility that you can see from town, it's called the Army Tower.
But, more importantly is of course are all of the various observatories that populate the mountain top.
Uh huh.
Doug, you know we keep hearing more about night pollutions.
Night pollutions, is that becoming a problem, light pollutions leaking out?
Well, at first came to a head here in Tucson even in the 1970s that the local city ,and county - Pima county had been a world leader and trying to limit that light pollution.
We've seen light pollution growing and get peak, but less than the population growth, which we regardless of positive sign.
Flagstaff has also been a world leader, now we're trying to work on Phoenix and get them - because we can actually see the lights of Phoenix from most of the major observatories in Tucson.
Are the towns actually cooperating and...
Yes and in fact...
In what way - how can you keep the light down?
Well, there's a recent example - people have probably heard about LED lights.
Yeah.
Unfortunately, the other part of the result has also occurred, and that is, the problem still exists.
This president needs to solve it, and the very - but the likelihood is that the intent is to leave that for the next president.

As you know, General Petraeus, encouraged by the vice president and I guess others, has already sounded the note that when he reports to Congress, I guess next month or so, he is likely to call for a halt in deployments.
That is, there is already beginning to be a draw down of the surge.
But he will call, I think, for a halt once the surge is completed and say let's wait a month or two before we take out any more.

And so we're likely to come into the election, with the next president facing the substantial question of delivering either - if it's one of the two Democratic nominees - on their promises to begin taking troops out.
I might say there are other commentators who are pointing out that typically with insurgencies of this sort, we are at about the middle of a normal tenure arch of insurgencies.
That is that this won't play out for another five years.
And if we have a strategy that says we're going to stay there until the insurgency is defeated, well, that suggests a likelihood of another five years, minimum.
Secretary West, when you think about the different competing interests that the U.S.

alone has in this to behave honorably - to support the Iraqi people, to ensure that America's strategic interests are protected, to protect the ongoing health of the armed forces as an institution and just on and on and on - and you think about the precarious nature of some elements of the U.S.

economy right now, with oil prices being extremely high, the housing market in turbulence, what will ensure or what could help ensure that this war does not actually get lost in a shuffle of priorities over the coming months?
Well, I think that was probably the best summation of all the conflicting pressures and that are making this pot boil that I've heard.

I don't think there's a danger that even as we begin to focus more and more of our attention as a nation and as a people and the attention of our politicians, those who are in power and those who are campaigning for power, on the economic distress of the country, I don't there is any likelihood that we're going to lose track of the war and the necessity to make decisions about that war, because to some extent, more and more, the two are becoming inextricably intertwined.
Are you surprised by any of the responses of the American people to this war over time?

Whether it's the initial support, the initial willingness to believe that Saddam Hussein and al-Qaida were interrelated, the falling support and what may now actually be a slight uptick in support for staying longer, are you surprised at all by how the American people who are at home, civilians, look at the war?
And how much influence - in your opinion, having had such an interesting vantage point - does the civilian opinion have on the progress of the military action?
We have a nation with some basically conservative impulses.
One of those - and it is a conservative impulse - is that when war looms, our populous tends to be reluctant to enter into war and into conflict.

But another part of the conservative impulse is to believe that when the chief executive of the United States, representing both the head of government and presumably all the organs that have information to bring to bear on a problem, but also head of state, which is all the majesty and stature of this national community, speaks on an issue and says it is important to do this thing, that even though the American people are not a credulous lot, they want to have answers and information, the inclination is to support our president if he makes a reasonable case and if the agencies under his command bring reasonable evidence forward.
And so, public opinion, though weary of a war, weary of a second war, could begin to move that way, understandably.

As the war's toll mounts, as there is less and less evidence that the successes that were claimed to be likely, then understandably, the American people begin to doubt the wisdom of the war and to doubt the commitment.
If you ask me the underlying belief, I think it is still a belief that this is a war we should not have entered.

It is a concern of people whom we've tried to help, but at some point, we have to ask the question, are we really helping in a way that will both contribute to their long term well being and allow us as a nation to fulfill our part in both for our people and in the community of nations in a realistic fashion?
If you were commander of the forces in Iraq, how soon would you want to see them leave?
I think another five years is way too much for us to expect of ourselves as a nation, of our service members and of their families.
I think that we have probably done what we can there.
And as the commander, I might well expect that at it is time to begin to prepare for a withdrawal, because a withdrawal, a draw down is a very difficult thing to implement.

By the same token, if you are a commander who has been invested in the mission there and whose belief that success is to not leave the battlefield or the theater, unless every single element that you set out as success has been achieved, then understandably, even I might find myself reluctant to leave as long as I could fashion arguments for my superiors that said, we're making progress here.
We are making progress there.

If I were making my decisions from the safety of back here in the United States, from a vantage point that says, I can see all the interests that compete for the attention of this country around the world, it might be a clearer decision and I might be much more inclined to say, as I am, we need to continue planning to depart from that theater.
Secretary West, thank you so much for your time.
It's been a pleasure.
Togo West, Jr. is the former secretary of the U.S.
Army.
And just ahead, our political experts size up John McCain's views on the war.

But as NPR's Laura Sydell reports, many experts say the case can be seen as a victory for Samsung and may mark a turn in the international battle between the two smartphone makers.

You can do multi-finger gestures on it.
And, boy, have we patented it.
ALLEN: There aren't a whole lot of congressional districts in America that have better beaches or better shopping than Florida's 22.
In Boca Raton's Mizner Park area yesterday, fountains bubbled and Cindy Newman took a break from her job at a nearby boutique.
What do I think about it?
I think it's sad.
Newman is worried about what may happen if the U.S.
goes into default.
But she thinks Allen West and other Republicans have been doing the right thing - insisting on big spending cuts as part of any deal to raise the debt limit.
All Obama wants to do is keep spending money.
Nobody wants to cut anything.
Everybody's for entitlements.
I have certainly had to cut back in the last four years and I expect them to too.
All I read about is how much money they make and all I'm doing is trying to survive.
A few miles away, outside a Whole Foods Market, Democrat Alan Rogoff(ph) says he's no fan of Allen West or at this point any elected officials from either party.
And I think the people in Washington don't give a damn about us, from top to bottom, OK?
Do you think there's actually a chance we're going to default here?
No.
They'll do something at the last minute and they'll all try to be heroes.
And like I say, none of them are my heroes.
